role_summary: Co-founder (1964) and Secretary-Treasurer of the Medical Committee for Human Rights (MCHR); civil rights physician with the MCHR's June 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer medical response; physician at the scene of the March 7, 1965 Edmund Pettus Bridge crossing in Selma ("Bloody Sunday"); fifty-year internist practice in East Harlem, New York.
ai_reading: Moldovan's top semantic neighbor is H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, the Tufts-Delta Health Center founder and fellow civil rights physician) at a high cosine count of 296, the catalog's clearest civil-rights-medicine pairing. The pairing rhymes a 1964 MCHR organizer with a 1965 Mound Bayou community-health-center founder; the embedding finds their shared movement-medicine vocabulary as the dominant signal, more than either's institutional affiliation. The novel observation his page surfaces is the documentation-as-physician signature: Moldovan was present at multiple foundational events of the movement (the discovery of the Mississippi Freedom Summer worker bodies near Meridian, the Edmund Pettus Bridge crossing in Selma) primarily as a medical-aid responder rather than as an organizer, and the embedding catches that observer-witness register over the more-photographed-as-organizer register the cultural reception applies to his MCHR colleagues.
biographical_paragraph: Alfred Moldovan's Civil Rights History Project oral history is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript. His external biographical record is documented through the Medical Committee for Human Rights institutional history and the Veterans of the Civil Rights Movement archive [src: 1, src: 2]. Moldovan was born in 1921 in the Bronx, New York. He attended City College of New York and Chicago Medical School, served in the U.S. Army Air Forces as a radio repairman during World War II, and completed medical school after his discharge. With Robert Smith, Aaron Wells, and others he co-founded the Medical Committee for Human Rights in June 1964 in response to the Mississippi Freedom Summer organizing's need for medical and emergency-response coverage; the MCHR sent physicians and nurses to Mississippi to treat injured civil rights workers and to provide community medical care in counties without Black physicians [src: 2]. He served as the MCHR's Secretary-Treasurer for several years. Moldovan was in Meridian, Mississippi when the bodies of Andrew Goodman, Michael Schwerner, and James Chaney were discovered in August 1964; he was in Selma at the time of the first March 7, 1965 Edmund Pettus Bridge attempt ("Bloody Sunday") and the subsequent crossings [src: 2]. He continued the East Harlem internal-medicine practice he had maintained for fifty years until his retirement in the 2010s [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Alfred Moldovan
interview_snippets.lead_in: Moldovan describes reaching the Edmund Pettus Bridge in an MCHR vehicle on March 7, 1965, as marchers came back over it, and being stopped by the sheriff:
interview_snippets.quote: And we got to the [Edmund] Pettus Bridge, and the people were streaming back over the bridge. And, uh, the sheriff was standing there, and he wouldn't let me cross. Uh, and I said, "The people are hurt! I've got to go there and help them." And, you know, "I've got to go!" Uh, and he says, "If you go from here, I'll blow your head off!" You know? And he wasn't kidding.

interview_snippets.speaker: Alfred Moldovan
interview_snippets.lead_in: On crossing the bridge after the troopers' attack to reach people who had been tear-gassed:
interview_snippets.quote: I was the only one that crossed the bridge and got to the other side and went into houses to drag people out, you know, who had been, had been gassed. I can still smell the gas.

interview_snippets.speaker: Alfred Moldovan
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the role of the Medical Committee for Human Rights in the South, which he describes as lending a presence rather than practicing medicine:
interview_snippets.quote: that, I think, sums up the whole essence of the Medical Committee for Human Rights: "We will be there."

interview_snippets.speaker: Alfred Moldovan
interview_snippets.lead_in: On why he opposed the MCHR taking a formal position against the Vietnam War, a split he describes with the committee's younger members:
interview_snippets.quote: But I didn't think the Medical Committee had a right to that, because there were people giving us money that were for the war. Now, I can't turn around and take their money and use it for things that they didn't believe in.

interview_snippets.speaker: H. Jack Geiger
interview_snippets.lead_in: H. Jack Geiger (entry 54), the founder of the Tufts-Delta Health Center and a fellow Medical Committee for Human Rights physician, names Moldovan among the committee's senior staff as it organized nationally:
interview_snippets.quote: A national ongoing medical committee for human rights was being organized with headquarters in New York. And I'm blocking Al Moldovan, Esther, a variety of other people as staff, senior staff and organizers.

gallery.alt: Alabama state troopers attacking Selma-to-Montgomery marchers at the foot of the Edmund Pettus Bridge, March 7, 1965
gallery.caption: Alabama state troopers attacking marchers at the foot of the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, Alabama on March 7, 1965 ("Bloody Sunday"). Moldovan reached the bridge that afternoon in a Medical Committee for Human Rights vehicle and, in his oral history, recounts crossing it alone after a sheriff threatened him, then pulling tear-gassed people from nearby houses. Federal Bureau of Investigation photograph from the National Park Service Civil Rights travel itinerary.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via the National Park Service Civil Rights travel itinerary)
